result:
The first time I really noticed the moon I was eight, lying on the trampoline my dad bought at a garage sale. It was October, the mesh cold enough to make me regret socks, and the sky had that black-jelly look, thick with stars like spilled salt. My mother had switched off the porch light so I could “get my fill,” though she didn’t believe anything was out there worth the crick in my neck. I disagreed then; I still do, only now the argument is quieter, internal, more like background music than a protest.  

I used to think growing up meant accumulating certainties: adding layers of conviction the way tree rings add girth. Instead I keep shedding them. Opinions, grudges, identities—the whole wardrobe of self—slip off like silk in reverse. The older I get, the less I understand, and paradoxically that feels like progress. Certainty is brittle; wonder bends.  

Take language. It is at once the most democratic and the most tyrannical invention we’ve got. Anyone can grab a few syllables, staple them together, and mint something the world has never heard, yet we’re hostages to every sentence we speak. The tongue builds cages faster than the mind can test the bars. When I was fifteen I wrote “atheist” on every social-media bio because I liked the slash of the vowels and the implied defiance. It took me a decade to realize I’d simply traded one set of expectations for another. Now I leave the field blank and feel lighter than punctuation.  

Still, words are my oldest companions. They wait like patient dogs while I fumble for keys, forgivable even when they bite. On bus rides I collect fragments of conversation the way kids collect seashells. A woman in lime-green scrubs says, “He asked if I still pray, and I told him only in the MRI tube.” A teenage boy with a voice like an unopened jar murmurs into his phone, “I can’t come home if home keeps shrinking.” I write these things on receipts, ticket stubs, the pale underside of my wrist, not for a novel I’ll never finish but because overhearing is its own form of care. To witness someone’s unguarded syllables is to hold a mirror to their atomized loneliness; we are briefly less alone.  

There’s a theory, maybe apocryphal, maybe quantum, that every choice you don’t make births an alternate universe: a child you didn’t birth, a city you didn’t flee, a mouth you didn’t kiss. If so, the air is crowded with parallel ghosts walking through us on their way to better restaurants. When I fail to send the text or hold the door or speak up at the meeting, I sometimes feel an almost-audible schism, like a zipper being tugged open just behind my peripheral vision. Somewhere, my braver replica is already living the next sentence. I envy him until I remember he’s probably envying me for the comfort of backward glances.  

Comfort, though, is treacherous. It mats around the heart like plaque, narrowing the valves. One spring I quit a job that paid in dental insurance and ergonomically certified chairs to spend three months sweeping floors at an artists’ retreat. I was lonelier than a Thursday newspaper, but at night I heard owls trading existential hoots over the marsh, and I learned the smell of linseed oil, which is, secretly, the smell of centuries whispering “keep going.” I’d never swept with intention before. Each stroke became a small erasure of ego—look, the universe allows you to disappear into service. By the time I returned to the insured world, my back hurt and my bank account coughed dust, yet I could feel hinges in my chest that hadn’t moved in years.  

I think often about the word “saudade,” Portuguese, untranslatable, a homesickness for something that may never have existed. Maybe that’s what we’re all orbiting: not the promise of heaven, or money, or followers, but the sliver of turquoise that flashes in the corner of memory when the freeway hits a certain angle of sun. The ache is the compass. If you follow it long enough you find yourself driving at 2 a.m. to a diner that used to be a train station, ordering coffee you won’t drink because you’re trying to taste a July night when everyone you loved was still alive and the jukebox had that Otis Redding song. You leave a large tip for the waitress who calls you “hon,” even though you hate endearments from strangers, because in that moment she is unintentionally nursing your saudade with free refills.  

People keep asking how to “be present.” I picture presence as a kind of shy nocturnal animal that will only approach if you pretend indifference: sit still, breathe normally, act like you’re not dying for a glimpse. Eventually it pads out, sniffs your palm, maybe allows a single heartbeat of contact before vanishing. The trick is not to chain it, photograph it, monetize it. The trick is to notice its whiskers and go on with your day, inwardly electrified.  

I’ve been practicing letting beauty upset me. Last week a thunderstorm cracked the sky open like a dropped watermelon; purple light, ozone, the smell of earth getting its first drink in weeks. I stood on the balcony and sobbed because nothing demanded I respond correctly. No status update, no affiliate link, no polite applause. Just voltage, rain, and the small humility of being drenched.  

My grandmother kept a notebook labeled “Good Things,” nothing more. She logged: the sound of a car door closing when you know it’s your ride home, the smell of gasoline on someone else’s shoes, the exact flicker of a neon sign that proves you’re not the last person awake in the city. She died with 2,347 entries. At the funeral my cousin read #1,191 aloud and the entire room inhaled as if we’d forgotten how oxygen tasted. That, too, was worship—no deity required, only the collective memory of being alive in the presence of someone who insisted on noticing.  

Maybe we’re not here to build perfect lives or credenzas of achievement. Maybe we’re just radios temporarily tuned to the frequency of existing, tasked with converting static into small, transmittable songs. I can live with that assignment. If tomorrow I wake up anxious, late, embarrassed by yesterday’s declarations, at least I’ll have the moon, which has never once apologized for its phases. Wax, wane, disappear, return—it just keeps moving tissue paper across the lamp of itself, perfectly indifferent and therefore trustworthy.  

Tonight I think I’ll step outside, barefoot for penance, and address it like my oldest friend: “Still here?” The answer will arrive silently, a silver coin slipped into the palm of the sky, and for a second—no longer—I’ll feel rich enough to keep going.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
